sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 19, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'moves player 4x4 avatar right by 6 cols to next chamber (VERIFIED steps 1,3,4)', 'DOWN': 'only shrinks HUD timer at [8,20] (step 2); untested at other positions', 'LEFT': 'untested', 'UP': 'untested', 'SPACE': 'untested'}
- Object taxonomy: {'blue_square_black_center': 'player avatar, 4x4 blue square with 1 black center pixel, 24 pixels total', 'blue_square_blue_center': 'target/goal, 6x6 blue square with 1 blue center pixel, 19 pixels, at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]', 'white_paths': 'maze corridors forming S-shaped path through black walls', 'red_path': 'red colored path on right side of maze, 82 pixels', 'white_square': 'white 5x5 square at [14,20]-[18,24], possibly another target or obstacle'}
- HUD map: {'row_63': 'blue timer strip, shrinks by 1 pixel per action (64→63→...)'}
- Win pattern: Reach the blue target square at bottom-right with the player avatar
Current level:
- World model: Maze with chambers connected by white corridors. Player is 4x4 blue square that jumps between chambers. Chambers in top row at cols 14, 20, 26, 32 (spaced 6 apart). White path forms S-shape: top-left → top-right → down right side → left → down → right to bottom. Player currently at [8,32] after 3 RIGHTs.
- Goal model: Navigate player from start (top-left) through maze chambers to blue target at bottom-right ~[49,43]-[55,49]
- Recent findings: ['Player moved: [8,14]→[8,20]→[8,26]→[8,32] with RIGHT', 'DOWN at [8,20] only changed HUD timer, no player movement', 'Player is now at [8,32] near red path area']
- Open questions: ['Does DOWN work at [8,32] (just tested, awaiting result)?', 'What is the full chamber connectivity graph?', 'Can LEFT reverse movement?', 'What does SPACE do?']
- Plan: Test DOWN at [8,32]. If it works, continue following the S-shaped white corridor path: down right side, then left, then down, then right to target. If not, try RIGHT more or LEFT back to find working DOWN position.
- Failed probes: ['DOWN at [8,20] - no player movement, only HUD timer changed (step 2)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 18 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT also didn't change the board (the player is still at [20,26]). Let me try DOWN and UP from here. But wait, looking at the image, the player appears to have moved! The blue square with black center is now at a different position - it looks like it moved left compared to before.
Wait, let me look more carefully. In the previous image, the player was at [20,26] and now it's still showing [20,26] in the output. But the image shows the player in a different position - more to the left.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the player (blue square with black dot) appears to be at a position that's more to the left compared to the previous image. But the code output says [20,26]. This is confusing.
Let me re-examine. The boundary[0] gives the top-left corner of the player's bounding box. If the player is at [20,26], that means row 20, col 26. But the image shows the player in a different position.
Wait, I think I misread the previous output. Let me check again:
- Previous: "Player at: [20, 26]"
- Current: The code hasn't printed yet, but the image shows the player moved left.
Actually, looking at the image, the player is now in a position that's more to the left. The white square that was visible before is now partially covered by the player. This suggests LEFT did work and moved the player.
But wait, the code output shows "Player at: [20, 26]" which is the same as before. This is confusing. Let me check the segmentation more carefully.
Hmm, actually I think the issue is that I'm looking at the wrong thing. The player's boundary[0] is [20, 26], which means the top-left corner is at row 20, col 26. But the image shows the player in a different position.
Let me just try DOWN and see what happens. If LEFT didn't work, I need to try other directions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# LEFT may have worked. Check player position and try DOWN.
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24 and n['children']:
            return n
    return None

p = find_player(current_frame.segmentation)
print(f"Player boundary: {p['boundary'] if p else '?'}")

# Try DOWN from current position
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "moves player right by 6 cols in top row, down by 6 rows in lower maze", "DOWN": "works at rightmost chamber [8,38] to move player down to [14,26]", "LEFT": "may work in lower maze", "SPACE": "resets player to start [8,14]"}, "object_taxonomy": {"blue_square_black_center": "player avatar (4x4)", "blue_square_blue_center": "target/goal"}, "hud_map": {"row_63": "blue timer strip"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at [20,26]. Testing DOWN.", "goal_model": "Navigate to blue target at bottom right", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT failed at [20,26]", "LEFT may have worked"], "open_questions": ["Does DOWN work at [20,26]?"], "current_plan": "Try DOWN, then map full path", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["RIGHT at [20,26]"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
